abstract.text: The witnesses you built in Tutorial 7 (replacement) and
Tutorial 8 (reparse) exist to make analysis caches
. Without translation every keystroke would invalidate
every memoized value — defeating the whole point of an
incremental tool.
A Cambium tree is meant to be queried frequently — by IDEs on
every keystroke, by analyzers walking every node, by formatters
emitting bytes. To stay within reasonable memory and ARC
budgets the primary traversal API is borrowed: walks happen
through
cursors handed to a closure, and source
text is exposed as a chunk-streaming view rather than a
materialized
Parallel parsing splits work across worker tasks: each
worker parses an independent subtree concurrently, then a
master builder splices the worker results into one logical
tree. The correctness-critical question is how the workers
share token-text vocabulary and the green-node namespace.
is
. Cambium’s red
layer realizes lazily under lock-free atomic slots, so two
tasks descending into different siblings of the same parent
never observe inconsistent state. The typed AST overlays are
also
, so passing a sub-expression to a
child task is just an argument pass.

private mutating func parseExpression(minPrecedence: Int) throws {
mutating func tryReusePrefix(at newOffset: TextSize) throws -> Bool {
guard let previousTree else { return false }
guard let oldOffset = Self.mapNewOffsetToOld(newOffset, edits: edits) else {
try oracle.withReusableNode(startingAt: oldOffset, kind: kind) { cursor in
guard let tokenCount = tokenCountMatching(text: cursor.makeString()) else {
return consumed == text ? index - currentIndex : nil
if newOff >= newStart, newOff < newEnd { return nil }
guard oldOff >= 0, oldOff <= Int64(UInt32.max) else { return nil }

abstract.text: Cambium’s CST stores everything as homogeneous nodes tagged
with a kind. That uniformity is what makes editing and reuse
cheap, but it leaves consumers to write
everywhere they want kind-specific behavior.
With the kind enum in place, you can write a recursive-descent
parser that emits into a
. The
builder accepts a stream of
calls and produces a green tree whose structure
mirrors the calls one-for-one.
Every Cambium grammar starts with a kind enumeration: one case per
token and one per node the parser can produce. The kind enum
becomes the
associated type of a
conformance, and Cambium specializes its generic types around that
language to make tree storage compact and traversal type-safe.

abstract.text: Cambium can serialize a tree to a compact byte stream and
decode it back. The format is length-, hash-, and
kind-validated at every record, so flipped bytes produce
named errors at decode time rather than silently malformed
trees.

abstract.text: A Cambium tree is meant to be queried frequently — by IDEs on
every keystroke, by analyzers walking every node, by formatters
emitting bytes. To stay within reasonable memory and ARC
budgets the primary traversal API is borrowed: walks happen
through
cursors handed to a closure, and source
text is exposed as a chunk-streaming view rather than a
materialized
Cambium can serialize a tree to a compact byte stream and
decode it back. The format is length-, hash-, and
kind-validated at every record, so flipped bytes produce
named errors at decode time rather than silently malformed
trees.
Cambium trees are persistent: there is no in-place mutation.
produces a
tree that shares as much green storage with
the old as possible, and returns a
describing the structural
change.
Cambium is a Swift-native concrete syntax tree library. This
tutorial series walks you through building a small but
production-shaped calculator language end-to-end: a
recursive-descent parser that produces a lossless tree, a typed AST overlay,
an evaluator with memoized analysis, persistent edits with
identity-preserving witnesses, an incremental reparse loop,
fork-join evaluation across structured-concurrent tasks, parallel
parsing with externalized interning, and on-disk serialization.
The parser from the previous tutorial handles only “atomic”
prefix forms — literals and parenthesized groups. To parse
correctly the parser needs operator precedence; to
survive
or
it needs error recovery.
Aggregate counters tracking how often the parser asked the
for a candidate and how often the oracle answered.

Cambium’s CST stores everything as homogeneous nodes tagged

Cambium can serialize a tree to a compact byte stream and
decode it back. The format is length-, hash-, and
kind-validated at every record, so flipped bytes produce
named errors at decode time rather than silently malformed
trees.

doc://org.cambium.Cambium/tutorials/Camb.title: Spawn workers against the shared interner

abstract.text: Parallel parsing splits work across worker tasks: each
worker parses an independent subtree concurrently, then a
master builder splices the worker results into one logical
tree. The correctness-critical question is how the workers
share token-text vocabulary and the green-node namespace.
